title: Dungeon Universalis - Creating Characters
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TS9rxa7-CXg

Daniel (@dungeondive) calls **Dungeon Universalis** the most robust character creation he has ever seen in a board game — rivalling some lighter RPGs. This video walks through the actual process by building two custom heroes.

First: **Bartok**, a renegade dwarven paladin and Champion of the Gods of Evil — a combination explicitly *not* in the suggested-pairings table, and he chooses it on principle. Eight development points get spent on combat (the first increase is one cheaper for paladins), movement, vitality, courage and strength. Skills come from his race (Tough, Cruel, Skilled Hands) and his class (Champion of the Gods of Evil grants a once-per-quest spell — he picks Inner Fire for the AOE damage). 20 starting gold buys a leather armour, a war hammer, and lockpicks. He realises afterward he forgot to subtract one from dexterity (paladins are penalised) and shrugs — "that kind of defeats the purpose of him being a dwarf, but I had fun creating him."

Second: a **shar-min wizard** — a race immune to poison, disease and offensive damage spells against natural armour, but with no armour permitted as a wizard class. Four random Light-school spells (Holy Light, Blast of Light, Clairvoyance, Sheltering Light), a staff and dagger, healing herbs, and the remaining gold spent hiring an elf mercenary with broadsword, short bow, leather, and Sharp Senses.

The walkthrough doubles as a tutorial in the suggested-vs-custom decision: ignore the suggested combinations at the cost of optimisation; embrace them at the cost of personality.

----

Daniel embraces the sub-optimal combination on personality grounds. When you create characters in any system that allows it — board game, RPG, video game — do you optimise the build, or build the character you want and live with the penalties?
